section.title: Faith or Works of the Law
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns from his own story to argue directly with the Galatians. He appeals to their experience, they received the Spirit by hearing with faith, and to Scripture, Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness. He shows that all who rely on works of the law are under a curse, and that Christ redeemed us by becoming a curse for us, so that the blessing of Abraham and the Spirit come by faith.
section.thesis: The Galatians received the Spirit and share Abraham's blessing by faith, not by works of the law, for the law brings only a curse on those who fail to keep it; but Christ redeemed us by becoming a curse in our place, so that the blessing promised to Abraham and the gift of the Spirit come to all who believe.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the appeal to experience, you received the Spirit by hearing with faith; the appeal to Abraham, who believed and was counted righteous, so those of faith are his sons; and the curse of the law answered by Christ becoming a curse for us, bringing the blessing and the Spirit.

units.label: The appeal to experience
units.note: did you receive the Spirit by works or by faith

verses.text: O foolish Galatians! Who has bewitched you? Before your very eyes Jesus Christ was clearly portrayed as crucified.
I would like to learn just one thing from you: Did you receive the {{pneuma|Spirit}} by works of the law, or by hearing with faith?
Are you so foolish? After starting in the Spirit, are you now finishing in the flesh?
Have you suffered so much for nothing, if it really was for nothing?
Does God lavish His Spirit on you and work miracles among you because you practice the law, or because you hear and believe?

units.label: The appeal to Abraham
units.note: Abraham believed God and it was credited to him

verses.text: So also, “Abraham believed God, and it was {{logizomai|credited}} to him as righteousness.”
Understand, then, that those who have faith are sons of Abraham.
The Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, and foretold the gospel to Abraham: “All nations will be blessed through you.”
So those who have faith are blessed along with Abraham, the man of faith.

units.label: The curse borne by Christ
units.note: Christ became a curse for us to bring the blessing

verses.text: All who rely on works of the law are under a {{katara|curse}}. For it is written: “Cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the Book of the Law.”
Now it is clear that no one is justified before God by the law, because, “The righteous will live by faith.”
The law, however, is not based on faith; on the contrary, “The man who does these things will live by them.”
Christ {{exagorazo|redeemed}} us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us. For it is written: “Cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree.”
He redeemed us in order that the blessing promised to Abraham would come to the Gentiles in Christ Jesus, so that by faith we might receive the promise of the Spirit.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, the Holy Spirit, the breath and power of God. Paul appeals to the Galatians' own experience of receiving the Spirit as decisive proof: the Spirit came by hearing with faith, not by keeping the law. The presence of the Spirit is the mark of belonging to God and the down payment of the promised inheritance, and it is given to faith, not to works.

logizomai.gloss: To reckon, to count, to credit to an account. When Abraham believed, God credited righteousness to him, a bookkeeping term: righteousness was reckoned to his account as a gift received by faith, not wages earned by works. This is the pattern for every believer, counted righteous before God on the basis of faith in His promise.

katara.gloss: A curse, the sentence of condemnation. The law pronounces a curse on everyone who fails to keep all of it, and since no one keeps it perfectly, all who rely on the law stand under this curse. The word sets up the stunning reversal of verse 13: Christ took the curse we deserved upon Himself.

exagorazo.gloss: To redeem, to buy out, to purchase and set free, as one might buy a slave out of bondage. Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, paying the price with His own death on the tree. Redemption is costly rescue: He took our condemnation so that the blessing and the Spirit could be ours.

basic.context: Paul is exasperated. The Galatians began the Christian life by faith and received the Spirit through the gospel, yet now they are being persuaded that they must keep the law to complete what grace began. Paul argues from their own experience and from Abraham that righteousness and the Spirit come by faith. Then he shows that the law only curses lawbreakers, and that Christ took that curse for us.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul reasons like a lawyer, stacking arguments: your experience, the Scriptures about Abraham, the curse of the law, and the cross. Notice how he reads the Old Testament as pointing to justification by faith all along. Read this as the case for grace built from the Bible itself.
basic.originalLanguages: The Galatians received the pneuma, the Spirit, by faith, not works. Abraham believed and righteousness was logizomai, credited, to him. The law brings a katara, a curse, on all who fail to keep it, and Christ exagorazo, redeemed us, buying us out of that curse by becoming a curse for us.
basic.typology: Abraham is the pattern of the believer, justified by faith long before the law was given. The curse of hanging on a tree, from Deuteronomy, finds its fulfillment in Christ crucified. The promise that all nations would be blessed through Abraham is fulfilled as the blessing and the Spirit reach the Gentiles in Christ.
basic.christ: Christ is portrayed as crucified before their very eyes, and He is the one who redeemed us from the curse by becoming a curse for us. He hung on the tree under the sentence we deserved, so that the blessing promised to Abraham and the gift of the Spirit could flow to all who believe. His cross is the hinge of the whole argument.
basic.god: God credits righteousness to those who believe, as He did with Abraham. God pours out His Spirit and works miracles in response to faith, not law keeping. God had planned from the beginning to justify the Gentiles by faith, announcing the gospel to Abraham. He is a God who blesses through promise and faith.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 15 records Abraham believing God and being counted righteous. Habakkuk 2 says the righteous will live by faith. Deuteronomy 21 speaks of the curse on one hung on a tree, fulfilled at the cross.
basic.oneStory: The promise to Abraham, that all nations would be blessed through him, drives the whole Bible forward. Here it lands: through Christ who bore the curse, the blessing and the Spirit come to the nations by faith. The gospel was preached to Abraham long ago and is now fulfilled in Jesus.

context.literary: Chapter 3 opens the doctrinal core of the letter with a barrage of rhetorical questions in verses 1 to 5, then shifts to scriptural proof. Paul weaves together quotations from Genesis, Deuteronomy, Habakkuk, and Leviticus into a tight argument. The structure moves from experience, to Abraham, to the law's curse, to the cross that answers it.
context.historical: The agitators likely used Abraham and the law to argue that Gentiles must be circumcised and keep Moses to belong to God's covenant people. Paul turns their own Scriptures against them, showing that Abraham was justified by faith before circumcision and before the law, so faith, not law, marks his true children.
context.canonical: This passage is a cornerstone of biblical theology, tying Abraham, the law, the prophets, and the cross into one account of salvation by faith. Paul's use of Genesis 15:6 and Habakkuk 2:4 anchors the doctrine of justification and shapes the argument of Romans.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove from the Galatians' own experience and from Scripture that the Spirit, righteousness, and Abraham's blessing come by faith and not by works of the law, and to show that the cross, where Christ bore the curse, is the ground of that blessing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The appeals to experience describe what God actually did among the Galatians. The scriptural argument establishes the abiding principle that justification is by faith, applicable to all people in every age, Jew and Gentile alike.

debates.question: Why does the law bring a curse rather than blessing?

views.label: Because no one keeps it perfectly
views.case: The law demands continual, complete obedience, verse 10; since all fail, everyone who relies on the law falls under its curse rather than earning its blessing.

views.label: Because relying on law is the wrong approach entirely
views.case: The law is not based on faith, verse 12; to seek life by doing rather than believing misunderstands how God justifies, so the attempt itself leaves one cursed.

debates.bearing: Both truths stand together: the law cannot justify because we cannot keep it and because it was never the path of faith; only Christ, who bore the curse, can bless.

debates.question: In what sense did Christ become a curse for us?

views.label: Substitution, bearing our condemnation
views.case: Christ took upon Himself the curse the law pronounces on lawbreakers, dying in our place so we could go free; hung on a tree fulfills Deuteronomy 21:23.

views.label: Identification with the cursed condition
views.case: Some stress that Christ entered fully into the cursed human situation; yet the text says He became a curse for us, which is substitutionary rescue.

debates.bearing: The gospel rests on Christ taking the curse we deserved so the blessing could be ours; the substitutionary reading is foregrounded and is the ground of our redemption.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's argument turns on four words. First, pneuma, the Spirit, whom the Galatians received by hearing with faith, not by works, which settles the matter by their own experience. Second, logizomai, credited, the accounting term used of Abraham, whose faith was reckoned to him as righteousness, the pattern for all believers. Third, katara, curse, the sentence the law lays on everyone who fails to keep it all, which means all who rely on the law. Fourth, exagorazo, redeemed, the costly buying back by which Christ became a curse for us, hung on the tree, so that the blessing of Abraham and the promised Spirit could reach the nations by faith.

typology.fulfillment: Abraham is the pattern and father of all who are justified by faith, his blessing extended to the Gentiles in Christ.

typology.type: Cursed is everyone hung on a tree
typology.fulfillment: The curse of Deuteronomy 21:23 is fulfilled in Christ crucified, who bore the law's curse in our place.
The promise of Genesis 12:3 is fulfilled as the blessing and the Spirit come to the Gentiles through Christ.

study.christ: Christ is set forth as publicly crucified before the Galatians' eyes, and His cross is the center of the argument. He redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, taking on Himself the condemnation the law pronounces on lawbreakers. Hanging on the tree, He absorbed the sentence we deserved, so that the blessing promised to Abraham and the gift of the Spirit could flow freely to all who believe. Everything the passage promises rests on this substitution.
study.god: God is the one who credits righteousness to faith, as He did for Abraham, and who lavishes the Spirit and works miracles among those who believe. From the beginning God purposed to justify the Gentiles by faith and preached this gospel to Abraham in advance. He blesses by promise, not by payment for works. In the cross He shows Himself both the just judge, whose curse must fall, and the gracious savior, who bore it Himself in His Son.

crossReferences.note: Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness, the text Paul cites in verse 6.
The righteous will live by faith, quoted in verse 11 to show the law never justified.
Cursed is everyone hung on a tree, fulfilled in Christ becoming a curse for us.
Abraham justified by faith before circumcision, the father of all who believe.

study.oneStory: God promised Abraham that all nations would be blessed through him, and the whole Bible presses toward that promise. It is fulfilled here: Christ bears the curse of the law on the tree, and the blessing of Abraham, along with the promised Spirit, reaches the Gentiles by faith. The gospel preached to Abraham centuries before finds its yes in Jesus, who redeems a worldwide family for God.

sermon.title: Some Crucial Questions (Galatians 3:1 to 14)
sermon.why: Begg follows Paul's pointed questions to the Galatians, showing that the Spirit and Abraham's blessing come by faith and not by works of the law, and that Christ bore the law's curse in our place to secure the promise.

quiz.q: How did the Galatians first receive the Spirit?

options.text: By hearing with faith, not by works of the law.
options.note: Verse 2 makes their own experience the decisive proof.

options.text: By carefully keeping the law of Moses.
options.note: Paul's point is the opposite; the Spirit came by faith.

options.text: By being circumcised first.
options.note: They received the Spirit before any law keeping, by faith.

options.text: By their own moral effort over time.
options.note: The Spirit was given to faith, not earned by effort.

options.text: Righteousness is credited by faith, so those who believe are his sons.
options.note: Verses 6 and 7 make Abraham the pattern of faith.

options.text: That circumcision is required for righteousness.
options.note: Abraham was counted righteous by faith before circumcision.

options.text: That only physical descendants of Abraham are blessed.
options.note: Those of faith, including Gentiles, are his true children.

options.text: That righteousness is earned by good works.
options.note: It was credited to faith, not earned.

quiz.q: Why are all who rely on works of the law under a curse?

options.text: Because the law curses everyone who fails to keep all of it.
options.note: Verse 10 cites the law's demand for complete obedience.

options.text: Because the law itself is evil.
options.note: The law is not evil; the problem is that no one keeps it fully.

options.text: Because God never gave the law at all.
options.note: God gave the law, but not as the way of justification.

options.text: Because the curse applies only to Gentiles.
options.note: It falls on all who rely on law keeping, Jew or Gentile.

quiz.q: How did Christ deal with the curse of the law?

options.text: He redeemed us by becoming a curse for us, hung on the tree.
options.note: Verse 13 states the substitution at the heart of the gospel.

options.text: He simply cancelled the law so the curse no longer exists.
options.note: He bore the curse Himself rather than merely cancelling it.

options.text: He taught us how to keep the law well enough to avoid it.
options.note: The rescue is His curse bearing death, not better law keeping.

options.text: He left us to bear the curse ourselves.
options.note: He took the curse in our place so we go free.

quiz.q: What was the goal of Christ's redemption in verse 14?

options.text: That Abraham's blessing and the promised Spirit would come by faith.
options.note: The blessing reaches the Gentiles and the Spirit is received by faith.

options.text: That we would finally be able to keep the whole law.
options.note: The goal is the blessing and the Spirit by faith, not law keeping.

options.text: That only Jewish believers would be blessed.
options.note: The blessing explicitly comes to the Gentiles in Christ.

options.text: That the Spirit would be earned by good works.
options.note: The Spirit is received by faith, not earned.

questions: Paul asks whether they began in the Spirit only to finish in the flesh. Where are you tempted to start with grace but then rely on your own effort to grow?
Abraham was counted righteous simply by believing God. How does it steady you to know righteousness is credited, not earned?
Christ became a curse for us. Sit with that exchange for a moment: what does it mean to you that He took the condemnation you deserved?
The Spirit is given to faith, not to law keeping. How have you seen God work in your life in response to simple trust rather than performance?
The blessing of Abraham now reaches all nations in Christ. How does being part of that worldwide family shape the way you see believers unlike you?

translationNotes.note: Credited translates logizomai, an accounting term; righteousness was reckoned to Abraham's account as a gift received by faith, quoting Genesis 15:6.
The righteous will live by faith quotes Habakkuk 2:4; the same text anchors the argument of Romans 1:17.
Cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree quotes Deuteronomy 21:23; Paul applies the curse of the cross to Christ bearing our condemnation.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 3:1-14. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg.
